elements.sent-2411-001: Dirac notation is widely used in quantum physics and quantum programming languages to define, compute and reason about quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper considers Dirac notation from the perspective of automated reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-003: We prove two main results: first, the first-order theory of Dirac notation is decidable, by a reduction to the theory of real closed fields and Tarski's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-004: Then, we prove that validity of equations can be decided efficiently, using term-rewriting techniques.
elements.sent-2411-005: We implement our equivalence checking algorithm in Mathematica, and showcase its efficiency across more than 100 examples from the literature.
elements.sent-2411-006: Dirac notation~\cite{dirac1939new , also known as bra-ket notation, is widely used by quantum physicists for representing quantum states and operations on quantum states.}
elements.sent-2411-007: For instance, a quantum bit is conveniently denoted as a linear combination $\alpha \ket{0 + \beta \ket{1}$, where $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{C}$ are complex numbers such that $|\alpha|^2+|\beta|^2=1$, where $|\cdot|$ denotes the real-valued norm of a complex number.}
elements.sent-2411-008: This notation is particularly advantageous for conducting complex calculations about quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-009: Dirac notation is also widely used in quantum programming languages~\cite{ying2016foundations, Paykin2017, Quipper, Lewis2023, Zhou2023,symQV to describe the semantics of programming languages and to write assertions, in particular pre- and post-conditions, about quantum programs.}
elements.sent-2411-010: An important consequence is that program verification of quantum programs involves extensive manipulation of Dirac expressions, similar to the way program verification of classical programs involves extensive manipulation of boolean formulae.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, a significant distinction between classical and quantum program verification is that in the quantum setting, there is limited support for automating these manipulations.
elements.sent-2411-012: Consequently, proofs of quantum programs are generally dominated by manual manipulation of Dirac notations.
elements.sent-2411-013: The lack of support for reasoning about Dirac notation has a negative impact on the verification of quantum programs: simple examples may be unnecessarily tedious to verify and larger examples can be extremely challenging to verify.
elements.sent-2411-014: To illustrate this phenomenon, \Cref{fig:HHL shows an excerpt of the proof in CoqQ~\cite{Zhou2023 of the Harrow-Hassidim-Lloyd (HHL) algorithm~\cite{Harrow2009 for solving systems of linear equations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In this example, assertions are written using Dirac notation, and each application of the unitary rule (to handle assignments) is interleaved with rewriting of the assertion using equational reasoning on Dirac assertions.
elements.sent-2411-016: The extensive use of non-trivial equational reasoning in program verification is a main obstacle towards scalable verification in quantum programs.
elements.sent-2411-017: On the other hand, quantum physics has a very strong mathematical foundation.
elements.sent-2411-018: Dirac notation is grounded in linear algebra and has a direct interpretation in Hilbert spaces.
elements.sent-2411-019: There has been a long line of work revisiting and developing these foundations from a theoretical computer science perspective, ranging from categorical semantics and models of computations to graphical languages and tools.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, these two lines of work have remained rather separate so far.
elements.sent-2411-021: In particular, categorical semantics does not target concrete language syntaxes and reasonings, while the ZX-calculus~\cite{vandewetering2020zx relies on a restricted form of circuit model.}
elements.sent-2411-022: We are not aware of any prior systematic exploration that treats general Dirac notation as an algebraic structure and designs the explicit automated deduction for equational reasonings.
elements.sent-2411-023: The main objective of the paper is to study Dirac notation from the perspective of automated reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, we consider the following questions:.
elements.sent-2411-025: What is decidable about Dirac notation?.
elements.sent-2411-026: Can some classes of problems be solved efficiently in practice?.
elements.sent-2411-027: What are the potential benefits for deductive verification of quantum programs?.

elements.sent-2411-028: This paper undertakes a systematic study of automated deduction for Dirac notation.
elements.sent-2411-029: The technical basis for our study is a presentation of core Dirac notation as a (many-sorted) first-order theory \DN\ that includes common objects and operators used in quantum physics, including bras, kets, operators, and different forms of composition and products.
elements.sent-2411-030: As such, it provides a convenient basis to consider decision problems.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our first technical contribution is a proof that the first-order theory of Dirac notation is decidable.
elements.sent-2411-032: The proof exploits the fact that every finite-dimensional complex Hilbert space is isomorphic to $\mathbb{C ^n$, where $n$ is the dimension of the space in which every formula of \DN\ is reduced to a logically equivalent formula in the first-order theory of complex numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The latter is decidable by a straightforward extension of Tarski's theorem, thus \DN\ is decidable.
elements.sent-2411-034: Unfortunately, the result is mostly of theoretical interest, in particular, due to the complexity of quantifier elimination---inherited from the reals and amplified by the decomposition of Hilbert spaces into Cartesian products.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our second contribution is a term-rewriting system for \DN\ expressions.
elements.sent-2411-036: Specifically, we define a term-rewriting system modulo commutativity (C) and associativity and commutativity (AC), and we prove, with the help of \CIME~\cite{Contejean2011 and \APROVE~\cite{Giesl2006 , two state-of-the-art tools for confluence and termination proofs respectively, that the term-rewriting system satisfies local confluence (modulo axioms) and termination.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: It follows that the equality of expressions can be proved by computing normal forms and checking the equality of their normal forms.
elements.sent-2411-038: As a contribution of independent interest, we also use the Coq proof assistant to give a mechanized proof of soundness for all the rewriting rules with respect to a formalization of finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces from~\cite{Zhou2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: As a further step, we extend our language with finitely indexed sums, a.k.a.,\ big sums, which are used pervasively in Dirac notation.
elements.sent-2411-040: For example, the entangled state $\sum_{i\in M \ket{i, i}$ is represented by the superposition (i.e., vector sum) of basis states.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We also define a heuristic procedure to prove the equality of extended expressions.
elements.sent-2411-042: We complement our technical contributions with three practical contributions.
elements.sent-2411-043: First, we implement a Mathematica package, called \DIRACDEC, that exploits the ideas developed in the paper in a richer setting with trigonometric and exponential functions, native big sums, and dynamic typing.
elements.sent-2411-044: Second, we evaluate our approach using more than 200 examples from the CoqQ project~\cite{Zhou2023 , which constitutes the main motivation for our work.}
elements.sent-2411-045: For completeness, we also carry a lightweight evaluation of \DIRACDEC\ on other classes of examples from the literature on quantum circuits.
elements.sent-2411-046: As expected, our tool performs less efficiently than existing tools that are specifically targeted to this class of examples.
elements.sent-2411-047: Third, we formally verify the soundness of our rewriting rules in CoqQ.
elements.sent-2411-048: We provide as complementary material the Mathematica tool implementation \DIRACDEC, the formalization of the soundness of the rewriting rules in CoqQ, the confluence proof in \CIME\ and the termination proof in \APROVE, and the set of examples.
elements.sent-2411-049: All will be made publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-050: This section provides a gentle introduction to Dirac notation.
elements.sent-2411-051: In order to be as self-contained as possible, we base our explanations on matrices.
elements.sent-2411-052: For more detailed accounts, we refer the reader to classic textbooks~\cite{Nielsen2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: It is very common to represent quantum states, or kets (denoted by $\ket{\cdot}$), as complex column vectors, i.e., as column vectors whose entries are complex numbers.
elements.sent-2411-054: For example, it is common to represent an arbitrary qubit as $\ket{\psi =\matrix{a b}$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Using this representation, it is easy to see that every quantum bit is a superposition of classical bits.
elements.sent-2411-056: Specifically, we can introduce the representations of the classical bits 0 and 1 as $\ket{0 =\matrix{1 0}$ and $\ket{1 =\matrix{0 1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Using basic properties of addition on matrices and multiplication by scalars, one can prove that $\ket{\psi = a \ket{0 + b \ket{1}$, i.e., every qubit is a superposition of classical bits.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The matrix representation of quantum states can also be used to represent covectors, also known as bras.
elements.sent-2411-059: It is very common to represent bras (denoted by $\bra{\cdot}$) as row vectors.
elements.sent-2411-060: The covector of a ket $\ket{v}$ is a bra $\bra{v}$ such that $\bra{v =\ket{v ^\dag$, where $\dag$ represents the conjugate transpose of a matrix.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Furthermore, bras and kets can be combined using matrix operations.
elements.sent-2411-063: One can, for instance, define the inner and outer product of two quantum states $\ket{u}$ and $\ket{v}$ as: \begin{equation}\<u|v\> \triangleq \<u| \cdot |v\> = \matrix{u_1^* \cdots u_n^*} \matrix{v_1 \vdots v_n}, \quad |u\>\<v| \triangleq |u\>\cdot \<v| = \matrix{u_1 \vdots u_n}\matrix{v_1^* \cdots v_m^* }.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-064: Inner product and outer product are the constructions that relate (co)states to scalars and operators.
elements.sent-2411-065: They are frequently used in applications, for example, decomposition state $|u\>$ as a linear combination of basis $\{|v_i\>\}$ is represented as $|u\> = \sum_i\<v_i|u_i\>|v_i\>$, and any operators can be decomposed as $A = \sum_{ij c_{ij |u_i\>\<v_j|$ for arbitrary basis $\{|u_i\>\}$ and $\{|v_i\>\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Unitary operators (describing the evolution of a closed quantum system), measurement operators (describing the quantum measurement), and Kraus operators (describing the evolution of an open system) are all represented by matrices, and the application to a quantum state is described by matrix multiplication.
elements.sent-2411-067: For example, Pauli $X$ gate, or bit-flip gate, is a unitary operator and can be represented as $X = \matrix{0 1 1 0}$.
elements.sent-2411-068: When applied to $|0\>$, the resulting state is $X|0\> = \matrix{0 1 1 0}\matrix{1 0 = \matrix{0 1 = |1\>$.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: When many-body quantum systems are considered, states in and operators on the composite systems are described by tensor products (Kronecker products) together with possible linear combinations.
elements.sent-2411-070: For example, for a two-qubit system with $|0\>$ and $|1\>$ on the first and second qubit, the whole system is described by: \begin{equation}|0\>\otimes|1\> = \matrix{1 0} \otimes \matrix{0 1} = \left[\begin{smallmatrix} 0 1 0 0 \end{smallmatrix}\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-071: Finally, the matrix representation is convenient for building complex quantum states from smaller constituents.
elements.sent-2411-072: For example, the maximally entangled state of the two-qubit system, known as the Bell state, is: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:bell state} |\Phi\> = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\>\otimes|0\> + |1\>\otimes|1\>) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left[\begin{smallmatrix} 1 0 0 1 \end{smallmatrix}\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-073: The matrix representation is intuitive and is widely used to compute quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-074: However, working directly with the concrete matrix representation has a number of shortcomings: \begin{inparaenum [1.] \item complexity problem: expressing and calculating matrices require time and space scaled by the Hilbert space dimension, which grows exponentially with the number of subsystems; \item for composite systems, structural information is lost if a state or operator is represented in a matrix form, e.g., for ~\Cref{eqn:bell state , the calculation from left to right is trivial while the decomposition from right to left is relatively less obvious; \item matrix representation is limited to finite-dimensional Hilbert space and cannot explore the properties of infinite-dimensional cases.\end{inparaenum These shortcomings are overcome by Dirac notation~\cite{dirac1939new , which provides an expressive syntax for quantum states and operators.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The syntax of Dirac critically exploits the algebraic structure of quantum states and linear operators as Hilbert spaces.
elements.sent-2411-076: Formally, let $\cH$ be a (finite or infinite) Hilbert space with inner product denoted by $\<\mathbf{u ,\mathbf{v}\>\in\CC$ for any $\mathbf{u ,\mathbf{v}\in\cH$, and we write $\cL(\cH,\cH^\prime)$ for the set of linear operators mapping from $\cH$ to $\cH^\prime$ (further abbreviated to $\cL(\cH)\triangleq \cL(\cH,\cH)$ for endomorphisms).}}

elements.sent-2411-079: Bra $\<u|$ is a linear mapping $\<u| :\cH \rightarrow \CC$ defined by $\<u|\triangleq\mathbf{v}\mapsto \<\mathbf{u ,\mathbf{v}\>$.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Such linear maps form a dual Hilbert space denoted by $\cH^*$ (which is isomorphic to $\cH$);.

elements.sent-2411-084: One major benefit of Dirac notation is that quantum states are concisely described by symbolic expressions rather than through their matrix representation.
elements.sent-2411-085: Thus, all equational reasoning about quantum states can be done completely symbolically and, in particular, independently of the dimension of the underlying Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-086: Thanks to its benefits, Dirac notation is widely adopted in quantum physics and in the foundations of quantum programming languages, as sketched below.
elements.sent-2411-088: Dirac notation plays an essential role in quantum physics and in the semantics and verification of quantum programming languages, as elaborated below.
elements.sent-2411-090: Dirac notation was originally designed to describe quantum mechanics~\cite{dirac1939new .}
elements.sent-2411-091: It can be used to describe quantum states as well as operations and measurements over quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-092: For example, the unitary evolution $U$ transforms an input state $|u\>$ to $U|u\>$; quantum measurement is a set of linear operators $\{M_i\}$ such that, performing on normalized state $|u\>$, we have probability $\<u|M_i^\dag M_i|u\>$ to obtain classical result $i$ with post-measurement state $\frac{M_i|u\> {\sqrt{\<u|M_i^\dag M_i|u\> }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The formalization of quantum computing and quantum information has gained attention in recent years~\cite{bordg2021certified, echenim2024formalization .}
elements.sent-2411-094: For example, the project~\cite{bordg2020isabelle titled "Isabelle Marries Dirac" formalizes the fundamental concepts of quantum mechanics within Isabelle/HOL and introduces Dirac notation into the library to facilitate expressions.}

elements.sent-2411-098: Generally, quantum programming languages include syntax corresponding to physical operations, which leads to the wide application of Dirac notation in each step.
elements.sent-2411-099: It includes defining semantics, proving general properties of programs and states via semantics, showing the soundness and completeness of Hoare-style proof systems, deriving and simplifying quantum predicates, etc..
elements.sent-2411-100: Entanglement is one of the most important phenomena that distinguishes quantum systems from classical systems.
elements.sent-2411-101: In quantum computing, entangled states serve as a crucial computational resource, particularly for distributed systems and communication protocols, and they also play a key role in quantum cryptography.
elements.sent-2411-102: In physics (and even in realms of philosophy), entangled states challenge our conventional understanding of correlation and causality through the quantum non-locality, e.g., quantum effect can propagate faster than light (although this does not imply classical information can be transferred faster than light) \footnote{This history of physics is particularly fascinating.}
elements.sent-2411-103: In 1935, physicists Albert Einstein, Boris Podolsky, and Nathan Rosen introduced a paradox concerning entangled states, suggesting that quantum mechanics is incomplete, i.e., it cannot be interpreted by local hidden variable models.
elements.sent-2411-104: In 1964, John Bell formulated the Bell inequality, which provides a way to experimentally test the existence of non-locality.
elements.sent-2411-105: To date, all experiments have aligned with the predictions of quantum mechanics, confirming that non-locality does indeed exist.. .
elements.sent-2411-106: This phenomenon might be demonstrated by a simple example: suppose Alice and Bob share the maximally entangled states $|\Phi\> = \sum_{i\in V |i\>|i\>$.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The following two actions are equivalent: 1.
elements.sent-2411-108: Alice applies an arbitrary operator $M$ to her state; 2.
elements.sent-2411-109: Bob applies operator $M^T$ (the transpose of $M$) to his states, which can be described by the equation $(M\otimes I)|\Phi\> = (I\otimes M^T)|\Phi\>$.
elements.sent-2411-110: This equation might be interpreted intuitively as no matter who performs the operation and how far they are separated, the global state changes simultaneously on both sides.
elements.sent-2411-111: Then for all operators $M \in \cL(\mathcal{H _V)$, \begin{equation}(M \otimes I) \ket{\Phi} = (I \otimes M^T) \ket{\Phi}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Here is a step-wise proof of how humans derive the equations, which proceeds by rewriting both sides into the same form according to the laws of linear algebra.
elements.sent-2411-113: The LHS is : \begin{equation}(LHS) \quad (M \otimes I) \ket{\Phi} \notag =\ (M \otimes I) \left(\sum_{i \in V} \ket{i} \otimes \ket{i}\right) \text{\{ unfold $\ket{\Phi}$ definition \}} \label{eq: motivating-example-1} =\ \sum_{i\in V} (M \otimes I) (\ket{i} \otimes \ket{i}) \text{\{ push terms into the sum \}} \label{eq: motivating-example-2} =\ \sum_{i\in V} (M \ket{i}) \otimes \ket{i} \text{\{ rearrange tensor and composition \}} \label{eq: motivating-example-3} =\ \sum_{i\in V}\left(\sum_{j \in V}\ket{j}\bra{j}\right)(M \ket{i}) \otimes \ket{i} \text{\{ insert the identity $I = \sum_{j\in V}\ket{j}\bra{j}$ \}} \label{eq: motivating-example-4} =\ \sum_{i \in V}\sum_{j \in V}\bra{j} M \ket{i} \ket{j} \otimes \ket{i} \text{\{ rearrange tensor and composition \}} \label{eq: motivating-example-5}\end{equation} We further proceed on RHS: \begin{equation}(RHS) \quad (I \otimes M^T) \ket{\Phi} \notag =\ \left(I \otimes \left(\sum_{i\in V} \sum_{j \in V} \bra{i}M\ket{j}\ket{j}\bra{i}\right)\right) \sum_{k \in V} \ket{k}\otimes\ket{k} \text{\{ unfold $M^T$ and $\ket{\Phi}$ definitions \}} \label{eq: motivating-example-6} =\ \sum_{k \in V} \sum_{i \in V} \sum_{j \in V} \bra{i}M\ket{j} (I \cdot \ket{k}) \otimes (\ket{j}\braket{i|k}) \text{\{ push into the sum, rearrange \}} \label{eq: motivating-example-7} =\ \sum_{k \in V} \sum_{i \in V} \sum_{j \in V} \delta_{i,k} \bra{i}M\ket{j} (\ket{k} \otimes \ket{j}) \text{\{ calculating compositions \}} \label{eq: motivating-example-8} =\ \sum_{k \in V} \sum_{j \in V} \bra{k}M\ket{j} \ket{k} \otimes \ket{j} \text{\{ eliminate $\sum_{i\in V}\delta_{i, k}$ \}} \label{eq: motivating-example-9}\end{equation} Then we check that normal forms ~\Cref{eq: motivating-example-5 and ~\Cref{eq: motivating-example-9 are equivalent under renaming of sum indices and the swapping of sums.}}

elements.sent-2411-114: Many similar properties of entangled states might be proved in the same style.
elements.sent-2411-115: However, this style of equational reasoning may quickly become challenging.
elements.sent-2411-116: In particular, equational proofs become significantly longer and hence more error-prone when more complex (bi-partite or even multi-partite) quantum systems are considered.
elements.sent-2411-117: Our work demonstrates that such forms of equational reasoning can be fully automated and carried out efficiently within existing tools for symbolic computation.

elements.sent-2411-119: A signature consists of a set $\mathcal{F}$ of function symbols with a map $\mid \cdot \mid$ that maps every function symbol to an arity of the form $(\sigma_1,\ldots,\sigma_n)\rightarrow \tau$, where $\sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_n, \tau$ range over a (possibly inductively defined) set $\mathcal{T}$ of types.

elements.sent-2411-126: In this paper we are concerned with the existence of decision algorithms for equations and formulae.
elements.sent-2411-127: A classic approach to decide if an equation is derivable is by defining a well-behaved term-rewriting system (TRS).
elements.sent-2411-128: Informally, the main difference between an equational theory and a term-rewriting system is that the latter is made of oriented equations, with the additional clause that every variable in the right-hand side appears also in the left-hand side.
elements.sent-2411-129: Given a set of oriented equations $R$, $e$ rewrites to $e'$, written $e\rightarrow_R e'$, if $R\vdash e\doteq e'$ can be derived using the rules of reflexivity and congruence arbitrarily often, and the rule of instantiation exactly once.. The reflexive-transitive (resp.
elements.sent-2411-130: We provide a brief recap of the properties of term-rewriting systems and the relations between the properties.
elements.sent-2411-131: A term-rewriting system is terminating, if there are no infinite rewriting paths.
elements.sent-2411-132: The terms that cannot be rewritten anymore are called normal forms.
elements.sent-2411-133: Another desired property is confluence, which intuitively says that the reachable terms during (possibly infinite) rewritings should not depend on the order of applying the rules.
elements.sent-2411-134: Formally, it means for all terms $u, s, t$ such that $u \to_R^* s$ and $u \to_R^* t$, there exists $v$ satisfying $s \to_R^* v$ and $t \to_R^* v$.
elements.sent-2411-135: There is a weaker concept called local confluence, stating that rewritings diverging by one step will finally converge, i.e., $\forall u\ s\ t, (u \to_R s \wedge u \to_R t) \to (\exists v, s \to_R^* v \wedge t \to_R^* v)$.
elements.sent-2411-136: For terminating term-rewriting systems, Newman's Lemma~\cite{newman1942theories guarantees that local confluence implies confluence.}
elements.sent-2411-137: In this case, there will be a unique normal form for every term.
elements.sent-2411-138: Some equivalence in our theory cannot be decided by simple term-rewriting.
elements.sent-2411-139: For example, transforming scalar commutativity $a + b = b + a$ into $a + b \reduce b + a$ will result in a non-terminating term-rewriting system.
elements.sent-2411-140: Therefore, we separate the problematic equations into the set $E$ and decide them using specialized techniques.
elements.sent-2411-141: Afterwards, we design the TRS for the remaining axioms $R$ and perform it on the equivalence classes defined by $E$.
elements.sent-2411-142: This approach is known as the term-rewriting modulo equational theories.
elements.sent-2411-143: The AC rewriting here is a special case where $E$ consists of associative and/or commutative theories of the symbols.
elements.sent-2411-144: To decide whether two terms $a$ and $b$ are equivalent in $R \cup E$, we can simply rewrite them into the $R$-normal forms and check whether they are equivalent with respect to $E$.
elements.sent-2411-145: The standard technique to decide the AC equivalence is to use auxiliary symbols with variable arities, then flatten and sort the successive nodes of the same AC symbol.
elements.sent-2411-146: For example, the two terms \((a + b) + c\) and \(b + (a + c)\) will be transformed into $+(a, b, c)$, whose syntactical equivalence implies AC equivalence of the original terms.
elements.sent-2411-147: Rewriting modulo AC also complicates the matching and unification procedure, which are essential components for rule applications and the local confluence proof.
elements.sent-2411-148: For example, the rule $\alpha + 0 \reduce \alpha$ matches the term $a + (b + 0)$ at subterm $b + 0$, while it can also match the term $(a + b) + 0$, which is AC equivalent to the original term.
elements.sent-2411-149: A classic approach to decide if a formula is derivable is quantifier elimination.
elements.sent-2411-150: Informally, quantifier elimination computes for every formula $\phi$ a provably equivalent quantifier-free formula $\psi$, so that derivability of $\phi$ can be reduced to validity of a boolean combination of atomic formulae.
elements.sent-2411-151: In this case, any algorithm for deciding equalities can be lifted to an algorithm to decide formulae.
elements.sent-2411-152: Naturally, the complexity of the decision procedure for formulae may be significantly higher than the complexity of the decision procedure for equations, when quantifier elimination is very costly.
elements.sent-2411-153: A celebrated result by Tarski~\cite{tarski1998decision establishes that the theory of real closed fields has quantifier elimination and is decidable.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Later, Collins~\cite{collins1976quantifier proposed a new algorithm for quantifier elimination.}
elements.sent-2411-155: This algorithm, coined CAD or cylindrical algebraic decomposition, is more performant than the original procedure by Tarski, but remains doubly exponential, so that many formulae in the theory of real closed fields remain beyond the reach of state-of-the-art verification tools.
elements.sent-2411-156: There is a long line of research that aims to extend Tarski's result to richer settings.
elements.sent-2411-157: The well-known Tarski's high school algebra problem asks whether the theory of real-closed fields extended with the exponential function is decidable.
elements.sent-2411-158: Our work builds on another, less pursued, line of research that extends Tarski's result to algebraic structures such as vector and Hilbert spaces.
elements.sent-2411-159: His proof is based on tools from stability theory.
elements.sent-2411-160: Their proof is based on an algorithm that transforms every formula in the theory into a logically equivalent formula in the first-order theory of reals.
elements.sent-2411-161: Its decidability follows from Tarski's theorem.
elements.section-2411-005: The Basic Theory of Dirac Notation
elements.sent-2411-162: This section introduces the basic theory $DN$ of Dirac notation.
elements.sent-2411-163: We start by defining the expression language.
elements.sent-2411-166: We use $\alpha$ for atomic type constants and Greek letters like $\sigma, \tau, \rho$ to represent the classical types.
elements.sent-2411-167: Types are built from classical types.
elements.sent-2411-168: Classical types are either atomic classical types or Cartesian products of classical types.
elements.sent-2411-169: Each atomic classical type $\alpha$ comes with a finite set of inhabitants $t_1,\ldots,t_n$.
elements.sent-2411-170: Given classical type $\sigma$, we define the type $\K(\sigma)$ of kets and the type $\B(\sigma)$ of bras, that will be interpreted as the Hilbert space spanned by elements of $\sigma$ and the dual of $\K(\sigma)$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-171: Moreover, given two classical types $\sigma$ and $\tau$, we define the types $\Op(\sigma,\tau)$ of linear operators from the Hilbert space spanned by $\tau$ to the Hilbert space spanned by $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-172: Finally, we consider a type $\Sc$ of scalars.
elements.sent-2411-173: Note that our type system does not explicitly include a constructor $\otimes$ for tensor products.
elements.sent-2411-174: Instead, tensor products are built through Cartesian products on classical types.
elements.sent-2411-175: For example, the tensor product of two types $\K{(\sigma)}$ and $\K{(\tau)}$ is represented as $\K{(\sigma\times\tau)}$.
elements.sent-2411-176: Next, we define expressions for bras, kets, operators, and scalars.
elements.sent-2411-179: We use lowercase letters like $s, t$ to represent the classical terms as bases, and $a, b, c$ to represent scalars.
elements.sent-2411-180: We use letters $K$, $B$, $O$ and their variants to represent terms from the ket, bra and operator sorts, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-181: Note that our expression language freely uses symbol overloading.
elements.sent-2411-182: However, overloading is easily resolved, as the different syntactic categories are mutually exclusive.
elements.sent-2411-183: Basis terms are constants, variables, or products of basis terms.
elements.sent-2411-184: Scalar terms are variables, constants $0$ and $1$, addition, multiplication, conjugate, the Kronecker delta function $\delta_{t_1,t_2}$ with $t_1, t_2$ being basis terms, and the inner product $B\cdot K$ with $B$ a bra and $K$ a ket.
elements.sent-2411-185: Ket terms are built from variables, constants $\mathbf{0 _\mathcal{K}$, and kets $\ket{t}$ where $t$ is a basis term, using addition, multiplication by a scalar, conjugate of a bra, operator application $O\cdot K$ with $O$ an operator term, and tensor product of two kets.}
elements.sent-2411-186: Bra terms are similar to Ket terms, except the conjugate of a ket and the operator application $B\cdot O$ where operator term $O$ appears on the right.
elements.sent-2411-187: Operator terms consist of variables, constant $\mathbf{0 _\mathcal{O}$ and $\mathbf{1 _\mathcal{O}$ indexed by classical types, using adjoint $O^\dag$, addition, multiplication by a scalar, composition, tensor product, and outer product $K\cdot B$ with $K$ a ket and $B$ a bra.}}
elements.sent-2411-188: We only consider adjoint $X^\dagger$ rather than conjugate $X^*$ and transpose $X^T$ in the core language.
elements.sent-2411-189: This is because the transpose can be constructed after we introduce the big operator sum in the following sections.
elements.sent-2411-190: Then, conjugate can be constructed from the previous two.
elements.sent-2411-191: Next, we define the type system for expressions.
elements.sent-2411-192: Typing judgments are of the form $\Gamma \vdash e: U$, where $e$ is an expression, $U$ is a type, and $\Gamma$ is a context, i.e., a finite set of typing declarations $y:U$, such that every variable is declared at most once.
elements.sent-2411-193: Typing judgments are implicitly parametrized by a map that assigns types to all constants.
elements.sent-2411-194: A typing judgement $\Gamma \vdash e : U$ is valid if it can be derived using the typing rules in ~\Cref{fig: DN typing .}

elements.sent-2411-195: The (Context) rule states that types of variables come from the context.
elements.sent-2411-196: The rest of the rules define the typing of each symbol.
elements.sent-2411-197: Rules from (Ket), (Bra) and (Operator) follow the requirements in their interpretations in linear algebra.
elements.sent-2411-198: For example, the typing rule for inner product $B\cdot K$ claims that $B$ and $K$ should be bra and ket in the same type of space, and the rules for additions require that only terms of the identical type can be summed up.
elements.sent-2411-199: The typing rule for three tensor products follows the isomorphism of product spaces.
elements.sent-2411-200: Notice that $\mathcal{O (\sigma, \tau)$ represents operators with domain $\tau$ and codomain $\sigma$, which is intuitively consistent with the order of bra-ket notation.}
elements.sent-2411-201: The adjoint of operators can be understood as the conjugate transpose in the matrix view, which swaps the domain and codomain.
elements.sent-2411-202: Types and expressions of \DN\ can be given denotational semantics using Hilbert spaces.
elements.sent-2411-203: We now turn to the interpretation of expressions.
elements.sent-2411-204: As usual, the interpretation is parametrized by a valuation \(v\), which maps all variables $x$ to their value $v(x)$.

elements.sent-2411-251: Our work considers a variant of term-rewriting, called AC-rewriting, that is tailored for equational theories with associative, commutative and associative-commutative symbols.
elements.sent-2411-252: Specifically, we define an AC rewriting system such that every expression can be reduced to a unique normal form modulo AC axioms.
elements.sent-2411-253: The existence and unicity of normal forms is established by termination and local confluence.
elements.sent-2411-254: We define a rewriting modulo system for Dirac notation as follows.
elements.sent-2411-255: For the equational part, we declare $+$ and $\times$ as associative and commutative symbols for all sorts and $\delta$ as a commutative symbol, which are listed below. \begin{equation}X + Y = Y + X \qquad (X + Y) + Z = X + (Y + Z) \qquad (for all sorts) a \times b = b \times a \qquad (a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c) \qquad \delta_{s, t} = \delta_{t, s}\end{equation} Some rules involving $X \cdot Y$ appear to be associative but are not included in the AC equational theory above.

elements.sent-2411-361: We have developed a Mathematica package, called \DIRACDEC, based on our equational approach for Dirac notation.
elements.sent-2411-362: The choice of Mathematica is motivated by practical considerations, which are detailed below.
elements.sent-2411-363: The long-term goal of our work is to propose practical automated tools for quantum program verification.
elements.sent-2411-364: As a consequence, our implementation goes beyond the scope of the theory developed in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-366: Mathematica has built-in support for trigonometric functions, exponential functions, and many other functions that are commonly used when specifying and reasoning about quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-367: Building our prototype on top of Mathematica gives us additional expressivity for free.
elements.sent-2411-368: In contrast, many examples from the literature are not expressible in the basic \DN\ theory and its extensions.
elements.sent-2411-369: Therefore, they would be out of scope of a direct implementation of \DN;.
elements.sent-2411-370: Mathematica allows to define new functions.
elements.sent-2411-371: For instance, one can define the function that maps every linear operator $A:H\rightarrow H$ to its trace \begin{equation}\sum_{i \in U(\pi_K(\type(A)))} \bra{i} \cdot A \cdot \ket{i}\end{equation} There are two interesting points to note about the trace.
elements.sent-2411-372: First, its definition uses big sums.
elements.sent-2411-373: Second, the index of the big sum ranges over the basis of $H$, therefore expressed by the dynamic typing introduced below;.
elements.sent-2411-374: Mathematica offers support for matching and substitution modulo C and AC, which can be used directly to implement the rewrite system used for DN;.
elements.sent-2411-375: Mathematica offers rudimentary support for interactive proofs, which is specially convenient for examples that fall outside of our core system.
elements.sent-2411-376: In spite of its advantages, the use of Mathematica as a backend is a trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-377: First, the performance of \DIRACDEC is constrained by the efficiency of Mathematica rewriting engine, and we can quickly reach examples that take long to verify, or time out.
elements.sent-2411-378: Further challenges with the choice of Mathematica are discussed below.
elements.sent-2411-379: We review some of the key features of our implementation.
elements.sent-2411-380: Although Mathematica has the big sum, it is designed for scalars and does not admit the equational theories for \DNE.

elements.sent-2411-384: Inspired by this, \DIRACDEC\ checks $\alpha$-equivalence by a constrained AC-unification.

elements.sent-2411-390: The definition of the trace operator depends on the type of the operator.
elements.sent-2411-391: There is no primary mechanism to define typed rewriting rules in Mathematica.
elements.sent-2411-392: Therefore, \DIRACDEC uses dynamic typing, in the form of an operator $\type{\cdot}$ that computes the type of an expression and can be used freely in expressions.
elements.sent-2411-393: For example, the trace symbol is formally defined as $\tr(A) \triangleq \sum_{i \in \mathbf{U (\pi_K(\type(A))) \bra{i \cdot A \cdot \ket{i}$ in \DIRACDEC.}}}

elements.sent-2411-395: Dynamic typing improves efficiency with respect to fully typed annotated terms because it avoids tracing the type of all subterms, and the rewriting rules will not match the redundant type information.
elements.sent-2411-396: The soundness of rewriting rules with dynamic typing is also proved in Coq.

elements.sent-2411-417: To ensure the reliability of the rewriting system, especially the implementation of dynamic type extensions, we formalize the Dirac notation in Coq and prove the soundness of all rewriting rules.
elements.sent-2411-418: Our implementation is based on the MathComp~\cite{mathcomp-analysis and CoqQ~\cite{Zhou2023 libraries.}}
elements.sent-2411-419: MathComp is a state-of-the-art mathematical library that provides extensive basic theories.
elements.sent-2411-420: CoqQ is a general-purpose quantum computing and program verification framework built on MathComp and MathComp-Analysis~\cite{mathcomp-analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-421: Other mathematical libraries related to quantum computing, such as \cite{RPZ17,HRH21 , also serve as the base library for our formalization.}
elements.sent-2411-422: We choose Mathcomp and CoqQ since (1) MathComp provides a comprehensive formalization of big operators and related theories, such as the lemmas about summation over finite sets; (2) CoqQ is compatible with MathComp and provides theories that relate classical data types and the associated Hilbert spaces.
elements.sent-2411-423: Based on these libraries, our formalization is directly built without further development of any relevant basic library.
elements.sent-2411-424: Our formalization consists of three layers, as listed below:.

elements.sent-2411-489: We observed that efficiency problems often arise when there are additions of many subterms.
elements.sent-2411-490: This is the case for quantum circuit calculations, where concrete gates are encoded as the addition of their decompositions on the bases.
elements.sent-2411-491: Such problems arise because our rewriting is powered by the general form of AC-matching (pattern matching modulo associativity and commutativity), which is a NP-hard problem~\cite{benanav1987complexity .}
elements.sent-2411-492: This can be avoided in practice, by using a compact representation and smarter matching algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-493: A large body of work explores the semantics and logical foundations of quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-494: A lot of this work is inspired by Abramsky and Coecke's seminal work on categorical foundations of quantum computation~\cite{DBLP:conf/lics/AbramskyC04 .}
elements.sent-2411-495: In this section, we review some of the most relevant related work.
elements.sent-2411-496: The ZX-calculus~\cite{coecke2008interacting, vandewetering2020zx is a popular graphical formalism that has been used in many applications related to quantum computing~\cite{vandewetering2020zx .}}
elements.sent-2411-497: The basic objects of the formalism are ZX-diagrams, which capture linear maps between qubits.
elements.sent-2411-498: As with other string diagrams, ZX-diagrams are naturally equipped with a notion of equivalence up to deformation.
elements.sent-2411-499: Further identities can be modelled by means of rewrite rules, that transform ZX-diagrams into simpler forms.
elements.sent-2411-500: A major question with the ZX-calculus is whether one set of rewrite rules is sound and complete for a class of circuits.
elements.sent-2411-501: This problem was studied extensively; see~\cite{vandewetering2020zx for a historical account.}
elements.sent-2411-502: An important milestone is the completeness of the Clifford+T fragment, which was established in 2017 by Jeandel et al~\cite{jeandel2018complete .}
elements.sent-2411-503: Further results prove completeness in more advanced settings, e.g.~\cite{Poor2023,carette2023complete considers completeness for arbitrary dimensions and superoperators.}
elements.sent-2411-504: Some completeness results, and some applications of the ZX-calculus to optimization are based on computing normal forms.
elements.sent-2411-505: For instance, Duncan et al.~\cite{duncan2020graph provide an algorithm to simplify Clifford circuits into a normal form that is asymptotically optimal in their size.}
elements.sent-2411-506: The ZX-calculus is used both as a theoretical tool, and as a practical tool, via implementations such as PyZX~\cite{kissinger2019pyzx , or certified implementations such as VyZX~\cite{lehmann2022vyzx .}}
elements.sent-2411-507: Path sums~\cite{amy2018towards is a representation of unitaries inspired by the path integral approach to quantum mechanics.}
elements.sent-2411-508: A path sum is an expression of the form \begin{equation}\ket{x}\mapsto \sum_{y\in Z_2^m} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^m}} ~ \exp^{2\pi \imath \phi(x,y)} \ket{f(x, y)}\end{equation} where $\phi$ is a phase polynomial, $f$ is an output signature, and $\mathbf{y}$ is a vector of path variables.
elements.sent-2411-509: This representation is closed under parallel and sequential composition, which can be used to interpret measurement-free quantum circuits as path sums.
elements.sent-2411-510: One advantage of the path sum representation of quantum circuits is that it equates structurally equivalent circuits without the need to use string diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-511: In addition, one can define reduction rules to simplify path sums into simpler but semantically equivalent ones.
elements.sent-2411-512: In particular, Amy~\cite{amy2018towards extracts a set of reduction rules from a careful analysis of the path sum encodings of some well-known identities for Clifford-group circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-513: One key property of these rules is that the number of path variables strictly decreases at each reduction step, which ensures that reduction terminates.
elements.sent-2411-514: Although these rules do not achieve unique normal forms for general path sums, Amy~\cite{amy2018towards proves that the uniqueness of normal forms holds for Clifford-group circuits, from which he derives that equivalence of Clifford-group circuits is decidable in polynomial time.}
elements.sent-2411-515: While this initial work is focused on Clifford-group circuits, the path sum framework can be used in other settings.
elements.sent-2411-516: Vilmart~\cite{Vilmart23 proposes a set of rewrite rules for Toffoli-Hadamard circuits, and proves termination and completeness of the rules via a translation to the ZH calculus~\cite{zhcalculus .}}
elements.sent-2411-517: Amy~\cite{amy2023complete considers the path sum representations with unbalanced coefficients, and provides complete equational theories over rings and fields.}
elements.sent-2411-518: Parametric path sums~\cite{chareton2021automated is a generalization of path sums.}
elements.sent-2411-519: Its goal is to model parametrized quantum circuits; the advantage of such circuits is that proofs can be established once and for all, independently of the size of the circuit inputs.
elements.sent-2411-520: Parametric path sums are used as a basis for QBricks, an automated verification tool that uses Why3 as a backend.
elements.paragraph-2411-019: Other Works on Completeness and Confluence
elements.sent-2411-521: One line of work establishes general completeness results for the internal language of the categorical structures, including symmetric monoidal categories, traced monoidal categories, and dagger categories~\cite{Selinger11,HasegawaHP08 .}
elements.sent-2411-522: Another line of work studies the confluence of quantum $\lambda$-calculi~\cite{Arrighi2017, LagoMZ09, LagoMZ11 .}
elements.sent-2411-523: Our work is closest to~\cite{Arrighi2017 , which introduces a linear-algebraic $\lambda$-calculus called Lineal.}
elements.sent-2411-524: Following Lineal, a sequence of work by D{\'\i az-Caro et al. further explores type systems to model the quantum computation concepts like measurements~\cite{diaz2017typing, diaz2019two and orthogonality~\cite{diaz2019realizability .}}}
elements.sent-2411-525: Verification of quantum programs has been an active area of research for the past two decades~\cite{CVLreview .}
elements.sent-2411-526: Ying~\cite{ying2012floyd establishes the first (relatively) complete Floyd-Hoare logic for quantum programs.}
elements.sent-2411-527: More recently, deductive program verification has been developed for various scenarios, including relational logic~\cite{Unr19,BHY19 , abstract interpretation~\cite{YP21, feng2023abstract , separation logic~\cite{ZBH21, LLSS22, singhal2022beyond, hietalaq, qafny , and robust reasoning~\cite{HHZ19, TSY21 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-528: These proposed proof rules are used for reasoning about quantum programs, often with the assistance of rewriting Dirac equations.
elements.sent-2411-529: Among these, the equational theory is crucial for deriving specific properties or equalities of programs, sharing a similar methodology (e.g., rewriting) that we adopt in this work.
elements.sent-2411-530: Mechanized approaches for quantum program verification have garnered significant attention; see \cite{LSZreview for a comprehensive review.}
elements.sent-2411-531: Numerous works aim to formalize the semantics of quantum circuits or programs from first principles and prove the properties subsequently.
elements.sent-2411-532: This line of research includes Coq-based formalizations \cite{RPZ17, Hiet21, hietala2020proving, Zhou2023 , as well as Isabelle/HOL-based formalizations \cite{LZW19, bordg2021certified, bordg2020isabelle .}}
elements.sent-2411-533: Dirac notation is used and introduced in several of these works to enhance readability and usability.
elements.sent-2411-534: Notably, CoqQ~\cite{Zhou2023 provides the first formalization of labeled Dirac notation to facilitate deduction when multiple variables are involved.}
elements.sent-2411-535: This paper proposes the first automated method for proving equalities between expressions based on Dirac notation.
elements.sent-2411-536: Our work lays the groundwork for future integration of automated methods into proof assistants and program verification tools, as well as the further development of automated reasoning for quantum physics and computation.. We elaborate below.
elements.sent-2411-537: An exciting direction for future work is to integrate our approach in a foundational verifier for quantum programs, in particular CoqQ~\cite{Zhou2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-538: Such an integration can take the form of a certificate checker that takes as input an externally produced sequence of rewriting steps, or of a reflexive tactic that provably computes the normal form of an expression.
elements.sent-2411-539: Existing libraries for term-rewriting such as COLOR~\cite{BlanquiK11 could serve as a starting point for our endeavour, but further extensions would be required to deal with \DN.}
elements.sent-2411-540: While there are several theories for the quantum Hoare logic~\cite{ying2012floyd, zhou2019applied, feng2021quantum , a significant challenge in practical application is the lack of predicate logic, i.e., the language to represent the predicates and reason with quantum operations.}
elements.sent-2411-541: Since these concepts are also described using Dirac notations, it appears to be a promising solution to integrate our theory and tool in program verifiers.
elements.sent-2411-542: For practical purposes, it would be highly beneficial to incorporate more sorts, more predicates, and finer typing.
elements.sent-2411-543: In particular, it would be valuable to define capture different classes of operators, and to introduce typed rewrite rules which capture the nature of these operators.
elements.sent-2411-544: An important direction for future work is to extend our formalisms and results to richer classes of big sums.
elements.sent-2411-545: Because such big sums are generally considered in the context of proof assistants~\cite{Bertot2008 , one challenge is to carve out a general yet tractable syntax for big sums.}
elements.sent-2411-546: One promising direction would be to take inspiration from work on array logics~\cite{DacaHK16,RayaK24 .}

elements.sent-2411-548: This section introduces the Dirac notation core language in full detail, including the dynamic typing technique, all the semantical definitions, axioms, and rewriting rules.
elements.sent-2411-549: We use $x$ for variables, $A$ for atomic type constants, and $b$ for basis constants.
elements.sent-2411-550: We use Greek letters like $\sigma, \tau, \rho$ for classical types and letters like $T$ for quantum types.
elements.sent-2411-551: We use lowercase letters like $s, t$ for basis and $a, b, c$ for scalars.
elements.sent-2411-552: We use letters $S$, $K$, $B$, $O$ and their variants to represent terms from the scalar, ket, bra, and operator sorts respectively.
elements.sent-2411-553: A context $\Gamma$ is an ordered list of typing assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-554: We write $x : X \in \Gamma$ if the typing assumption is contained in context $\Gamma$, and $x \in \Gamma$ if there exists a typing assumption of $x$ contained in $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-556: One is $\mathcal{WF (\Gamma)$, meaning that a context is well-formed.}
elements.sent-2411-557: And the second one is a typing judgement written as $\Gamma \vdash e : X$, indicating that $e$ is an expression of type $X$ in context $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-558: We say the term $e$ is a well-typed term in context $\Gamma$ if $\Gamma \vdash e : X$ is a typing judgement for some $X$.
elements.sent-2411-559: Well-formed contexts can be constructed starting with an empty context, and continuously appending unique variables with proven typing judgements.
elements.sent-2411-560: Basis types and Dirac types as terms have their types in kinds.
elements.sent-2411-561: The semantics of types are defined as domains and should be contained in the semantics of kinds.
elements.sent-2411-562: Therefore, kinds are interpreted as sets of domains.
elements.sent-2411-563: Atom is interpreted as the set $\mathcal{A}$ of all different domains from the atomic classical types, and the kind $P_1 * P_2$ contains all different Cartesian products from $P_1$ and $P_2$.
elements.sent-2411-564: The semantics of Ket, Bra and Operator are defined as the set of all Hilbert spaces, dual spaces and linear operators, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-565: Because we only consider well-typed terms, the semantics are always well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-566: The semantics of symbols in basis sort are defined as follows: \begin{equation}(Atomic Basis) \sem{b} \equiv \sem{b}_A, (Pairing) \sem{(t_1, t_2)} \equiv (\sem{t_1}, \sem{t_2}).\end{equation}.
